meta.title: Scholarship Digital Technologies (93604) – OMIS Indicators
meta.purpose: Indicators for algorithmic comprehension, development, reflection, and communication in Scholarship Digital Technologies

key_terms.definition: Understanding problem requirements, decomposing into subproblems, and explaining algorithm structure.
Designing and coding solutions that satisfy constraints, are correct, efficient, and well structured.
Evaluating correctness, efficiency, trade-offs, scalability, and suggesting refinements or alternatives.
Explaining design rationale, structure, and reasoning clearly and convincingly.

omis.description: Explicitly identifies inputs, outputs, constraints (ranges, limits), and stopping conditions before algorithm design or modification.

positive_signals: Formal variable names for inputs and outputs (e.g., N, result)
Description of output format (e.g., minimal number)
negative_signals: Mere restatement of the problem without specifics
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Restating the problem is not sufficient; explicit specifications are required.

success_examples: “Input: N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10⁶). Output: minimal number of moves.”
“Terminate when left > right; no built-in reverse allowed.”

omis.description: Breaks the logic into explicit control structures (sequence, selection, iteration, recursion) with clear hierarchy.

positive_signals: Use of IF statements with proper conditions
FOR or WHILE loops with clear bounds
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Bullet points alone are weak; explicit control structures are needed.

success_examples: “FOR i FROM 0 TO n-1 DO … ENDFOR”
“IF a[i] ≠ a[n-i-1] THEN return false ENDIF”

omis.description: Explains how subcomponents (loops, functions, variables) interact to meet the overall goal.

positive_signals: Phrases explaining how parts interact (e.g., 'first compute, then slide')
Description of data flow between components
Explanation of how loops/functions contribute to goal
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Listing components is not enough; interaction must be explained.

success_examples: “Outer loop moves window; inner loop updates counts; then test against threshold.”

omis.description: Design choices (algorithms, data structures) are explicitly justified with reference to requirements, constraints or limits.

positive_signals: Reference to constraints (e.g., 'because N is large, use sort')
evidence_guide.common_confusion: General statements without reference to specs are weak.

success_examples: “Because N can be up to 10⁵, I choose O(n log n) sort rather than O(n²).”

omis.description: Core algorithmic logic (loops, conditions, data updates) is implemented correctly, with proper returns and consistency.

evidence_guide.common_confusion: Implementation must match the design exactly.

success_examples: “While left ≤ right do … adjust pointers … return final result.”

omis.description: Explicitly handles boundary / edge cases (empty input, minimal size, duplicates) with guard logic or special cases.

evidence_guide.common_confusion: Mentioning testing is not sufficient; code must show handling.

success_examples: “When left > right break loop or return default.”

omis.description: Does not use disallowed built-in shortcuts (e.g. reverse, sort) in improvement tasks; logic is shown explicitly.

positive_signals: Manual implementation instead of banned calls
evidence_guide.common_confusion: In improvement tasks, built-ins are banned; show logic explicitly.

success_examples: “I manually compare s[i] and s[n-1-i] instead of using s.reverse().”

omis.description: Subcomponents and optimisations are integrated into a coherent whole; all parts feed into the final solution.

evidence_guide.common_confusion: All parts must integrate into the solution.

success_examples: “Helper function called in main loop; no redundant code; all pathways return a result.”

omis.description: Evaluates both correctness and computational efficiency, with justified reasoning, using Big-O or metrics and comparisons.

positive_signals: Statements like 'runs in O(n log n)'
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Claims must be supported with Big-O or comparisons.

success_examples: “Original was O(n²); my version is O(n log n) by replacing nested loops with sorting + two pointers.”

omis.description: Discusses trade-offs (time vs space, simplicity vs performance), limitations, and implications, comparing at least two methods.

evidence_guide.common_confusion: Must compare at least two methods.

success_examples: “Hash map is fast but more memory; tree uses less memory but slower operations.”

omis.description: Explicitly reasons about how the algorithm scales with large input (e.g. doubling N) using Big-O or asymptotic thinking.

positive_signals: Phrases like 'if n doubles, runtime quadruples'
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Must use Big-O or asymptotic reasoning.

success_examples: “At n = 10⁶, O(n²) is 10¹² operations — infeasible; my O(n log n) handles large N.”

omis.description: Adapts algorithmic reasoning to novel or variant scenarios, showing abstraction and insight beyond the base problem.

evidence_guide.common_confusion: Must show insight in novel scenarios.

success_examples: “If weights could be negative, I would use Bellman-Ford instead of Dijkstra.”

omis.description: Design steps, rationale, and reasoning are communicated clearly and convincingly, linked tightly to problem details.

evidence_guide.common_confusion: Explanations must be contextual, not abstract.

success_examples: “I chose merge sort because in worst-case it’s O(n log n), ensuring performance on large data unlike quicksort’s possible O(n²).”
